content.text: PowerShell Scripting with Az

Scripting the Az module well means more than stringing cmdlets together. A well-structured PowerShell script declares its requirements, accepts typed parameters, authenticates once, handles errors with try/catch, and returns structured objects that callers can compose. This section covers the patterns that make Az PowerShell scripts production-ready.

attrs.alt: Anatomy of an Az PowerShell script showing the five key sections

content.text: Script Requirements and Param Block
The #Requires statement at the top of a script is enforced by PowerShell before any code runs. It prevents confusing runtime errors when the wrong PowerShell version or a missing module is used.

#Requires -Version 7.0
#Requires -Modules @{ModuleName='Az.Accounts'; ModuleVersion='2.13.0'},
                   @{ModuleName='Az.Compute';  ModuleVersion='6.0.0'}

<#
.SYNOPSIS
    Provisions a standard VM in an existing resource group.
.PARAMETER ResourceGroupName
    Target resource group (must exist).
.PARAMETER VMName
    Name for the new VM.
.PARAMETER VMSize
    VM SKU. Defaults to Standard_B2s.
#>
param(
    [Parameter(Mandatory, HelpMessage="Existing resource group name")]
    [ValidateNotNullOrEmpty()]
    [string]$ResourceGroupName,

    [Parameter(Mandatory)]
    [ValidatePattern("^[a-zA-Z][a-zA-Z0-9-]{2,14}$")]
    [string]$VMName,

    [ValidateSet("Standard_B2s","Standard_D2s_v5","Standard_D4s_v5")]
    [string]$VMSize = "Standard_B2s",

    [ValidateSet("eastus","westus2","westeurope","southeastasia")]
    [string]$Location = "eastus"
)

ValidateSet, ValidatePattern, and ValidateRange run before any script code executes — they catch invalid input early with a clear error message, and PowerShell tab-completes ValidateSet values automatically.
Functions and Modularity
Functions in PowerShell scripts follow the same Verb-Noun convention as cmdlets. Keeping Az logic in named functions makes scripts testable with Pester and readable at a glance.
Error Handling Patterns
Az cmdlets throw terminating errors when used with -ErrorAction Stop. Combine that with try/catch to distinguish resource-not-found from authentication errors from throttling.
Returning Structured Output
Scripts that return [PSCustomObject] instances compose cleanly with other PowerShell commands. Callers can filter, export, or pass output to the next script in a pipeline.
Script Modules (.psm1)
When Az helper functions are reused across multiple scripts, extract them into a PowerShell module (.psm1 file). A module is imported once per session and its functions are available throughout.

# Using the module in another script:
Import-Module ".\AzHelpers.psm1"

# SupportsShouldProcess enables -WhatIf support automatically:
Invoke-IdempotentResourceGroup -Name myRG -Location eastus -WhatIf
# Output: What if: Performing the operation "Create resource group" on target "myRG".

# Actual execution:
Invoke-IdempotentResourceGroup -Name myRG -Location eastus -Tags @{Env="Prod"}
Working with Credentials and Secrets in Scripts

# Never hardcode secrets — read from environment variables or Key Vault

# Pattern 1: Environment variables (set by CI/CD secret store)
$clientSecret = $env:AZURE_CLIENT_SECRET
if (-not $clientSecret) { throw "AZURE_CLIENT_SECRET is not set" }

# Pattern 2: Key Vault (requires an authenticated context first)
function Get-SecretFromKV {
    param([string]$VaultName, [string]$SecretName)
    $secret = Get-AzKeyVaultSecret -VaultName $VaultName -Name $SecretName -AsPlainText
    if (-not $secret) { throw "Secret '$SecretName' not found in vault '$VaultName'" }
    return $secret
}

$connectionString = Get-SecretFromKV -VaultName "corp-kv" -Name "db-connection-string"

# Pattern 3: Managed identity + Key Vault reference (zero secrets in script)
Connect-AzAccount -Identity | Out-Null
$dbConn = Get-SecretFromKV -VaultName "corp-kv" -Name "db-connection-string"
# The managed identity's RBAC permissions determine what secrets it can read
Testing Az Scripts with Pester
Pester is the standard PowerShell testing framework. Az cmdlets can be mocked so tests run without connecting to Azure or incurring costs.

# Run the tests
Invoke-Pester -Path ".\AzHelpers.Tests.ps1" -Output Detailed

# Run with code coverage
Invoke-Pester -Path ".\AzHelpers.Tests.ps1" `
    -CodeCoverage ".\AzHelpers.psm1" `
    -CodeCoverageOutputFile coverage.xml
Script Execution Policy
PowerShell's execution policy controls which scripts are allowed to run. In production automation, you set the policy explicitly rather than relying on the default.
Policy
What Runs
Typical Use
Restricted
No scripts (cmdlets only)
Default on Windows client
AllSigned
Only scripts signed by trusted publisher
Highly secure environments
RemoteSigned
Local scripts run; downloaded scripts need signature
Common for dev workstations
Unrestricted
All scripts run (prompt for downloaded)
macOS/Linux default
Bypass
Nothing blocked, no warnings
CI/CD runners (set per-process)

# Check current policy
Get-ExecutionPolicy -List

# Set for the current process only (CI/CD — does not persist)
Set-ExecutionPolicy Bypass -Scope Process -Force

# Set for current user (persists, no admin needed)
Set-ExecutionPolicy RemoteSigned -Scope CurrentUser

# In GitHub Actions / Azure DevOps — set in the pipeline:
# shell: pwsh
# run: |
#   Set-ExecutionPolicy Bypass -Scope Process -Force
#   .\scripts\deploy.ps1
Idempotent Provisioning Script: End-to-End Example

The following script demonstrates all patterns combined: typed parameters, idempotency guards, Key Vault secret retrieval, structured output, and clean error handling. It provisions a storage account and writes a connection string back to Key Vault.
Scheduled and Long-Running Scripts

Some Az automation tasks run on a schedule (nightly cleanup, weekly reports) or wait for an async operation to complete. PowerShell provides Start-Sleep, jobs, and the Az -AsJob pattern for these cases.
Logging and Auditing Script Runs

Automation scripts that modify resources should emit structured logs so their actions are auditable. Combine Write-Host output with Azure Monitor or a local log file depending on where the script runs.
Summary

Production Az PowerShell scripts follow a clear structure: #Requires declarations at the top, a typed param block with validation attributes, a single authentication call (Disable-AzContextAutosave + Connect-AzAccount), core logic in try/catch blocks with -ErrorAction Stop, and [PSCustomObject] return values for composability. Extract reusable functions into .psm1 modules. Mock Az cmdlets in Pester tests so they run without live Azure credentials. Set execution policy per-process in CI/CD using Set-ExecutionPolicy Bypass -Scope Process.
